questions.text: Answer parts (a), (b) and (c). (a) Write a brief summary of the comparison and legislative powers of each of the following Union institutions: (i) the European Parliament; (ii) the European Council; (iii) the Council; and (iv) the European Commission. (b) In light of your answer in (a), explain whether there is separation of legislative and executive powers in the EU? (c) Outline the role of national parliaments in the EU legislative process, as provided for in Article 12 TEU and Protocol Nos. 1 and 2 to the Treaty of Lisbon. What is the rationale for according national parliaments this role?

Answer parts (a) [or]{.underline} (b). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (a) "The position of directives is very complicated" (Craig & De Burca, EU law, 4^th^ ed. (2008) at p. 303) Explain the sources of this complexity in the case-law on the direct effect of directives. Should directives be accorded horizontal direct effect? Or (b) On 1 February 2010, a planning authority in the (fictitious) Member State of Boomtonia, granted Windy Ltd 'special planning permission' ('SPP') for construction of a windfarm. No 'environmental detriment assessment' ('EDA') was carried out in respect of Windy's application, as none was required under the planning law of the Boomtonia. On 1 July 2010, the Court of Justice decided in the (fictitious) case of Commission v Boomtonia that the Boomtonian planning practice of granting a SPP without an EDA was contrary to the (fictitious) EU Assessment of Environmental Damage Directive, the deadline for the transposition of which into national law was 1 February 2009. Considering its planning permission to be fully valid as a matter of national law, Windy Ltd is still proposing to build the wind farm. The Green Alliance, a group of local residents opposed to the development, wishes to have the planning authority's decision annulled by the national courts. Moreover, Mr Farmer, a local farmer, would like compensation for the probable reduction in the value of his farm that would be occasioned by the construction of the windfarm, which there is evidence to suggest could render it prone to landslides in times of heavy rainfall. Advise the Green Alliance whether it could rely on: (i) the EDA Directive against the planning authority in judicial review proceedings in Boomtonia seeking to have grant of SPP quashed; (ii) the EDA Directive in injunctive proceedings against Windy Ltd seeking to stop the development. Advise Mr Farmer whether, if the wind farm is built, he could obtain, in legal proceedings brought in Boomtonia alleging breach of EU law: (iii) damages against Boomtonia; (iv) damages against Windy Ltd.

Answer part (a) [or]{.underline} (b). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (a) "One of the novel features of the Community legal order is the power given to the Commission to supervise compliance by the Member States with their obligations under the Treaties." (Audretsch, Supervision in European Community Law 2^nd^ ed, 1986) Appraise the procedures under Articles 258 to 260 TFEU (formerly Articles 226 to 228 EC) by reference to relevant Court of Justice case-law and from the perspective of their contribution to securing the Member States' compliance with their obligations under EU law. From this perspective, evaluate the changes introduced by successive Treaty reforms, including the Treaty of Lisbon. Or (b) Analyse by reference to relevant case-law the interpretation by the Union judicature of the standing requirements of "direct and individual concern" under the fourth paragraph of Article 263 TFEU (formerly Article 230 EC). Do this standing requirement and the Lisbon Treaty reform thereto ensure effective judicial protection?

questions.description: Write a note on [any three]{.underline} of the following seven cases, setting out their core facts, the key issues of…
questions.text: Write a note on [any three]{.underline} of the following seven cases, setting out their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Union judicature: (i) Case 106/77 Simmenthal [1978] ECR 629; (ii) Case 152/84 Marshall v Southampton AHA (No. 1) [1986] ECR 723; (iii) Case T-342/99 Airtours v Commission [2002] ECR II-2585; (iv) Case C-280/00 Altmark Trans and Regierungspräsidium Magdeburg [2003] ECR I- 7747; (v) Case C-224/01 Köbler [2003] ECR I-10239; (vi) Case T-201/04 Microsoft v Commission [2007] ECR II-3601; (vii) Joined Cases C-402/06 P and C-415/05 P Kadi and Al Barakaat International Foundation v Council [2008] ECR I-6351.

questions.description: Assess the compatibility of [each]{.underline} of the following…
questions.text: Assess the compatibility of [each]{.underline} of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with EU law and in particular with Articles 28 and 30 TFEU, Articles 34 and 36 TFEU, Article 49 TFEU and Article 10 TFEU (formerly Articles 23 and 25 EC, Articles 28 and 30 EC, Article 43 EC and Article 90 EC): (i) A tax by Mayo County Council on the transportation of natural gas extracted off the Mayo coast and processed within the Council's functional area out of that area, with the result that only gas extracted and used in Mayo in that area is not subject to the charge. Mayo County Council says it is justified because local gas uses pay rates, other local charges and /or have to tolerate the location of a massive gas refinery in the country; (ii) A provision in the Corrective Lenses Act which authorises the sale of contract lenses solely in shops which specialise in the supply of medical devices, such as opticians. The Government says that the measure is necessary in the interest of protecting human health to ensure that potential purchasers of contact lenses get proper advice regarding the desirability of having their eyes tested first and their suitability for wearing contact lenses; (iii) A provision in the Pharmacy Acts which prohibits the owning, operation or control of pharmacies by persons not having the status of pharmacist and limiting the number of pharmacies that any individual pharmacist may own, operate or control to a maximum of three, all of which must be located within a 50-kilometre radius of each other. The Government says that these measures are necessary to protect consumers by rendering individual pharmacists fully responsible for products sold in chemists and reducing the likelihood that the maximisation of profit margins might overly influence their advice to consumers.

Answer parts (i), (ii) and (iii). (i) Mary is a dual British and Irish national, who has lived all her life in the United Kingdom. She marries Bob, a Jamaican national, whilst on holiday in Jamaica. Bob is refused a right of residence in the UK under UK immigration law. Advise Mary and Bon on whether EU law affords them a right of residence in the UK. (ii) Olivier is a trainee player with a French football club, who has been offered a one-year professional contract with that club. He has also received a two-year offer from an English premiership club, which he wishes to accept. Under a French Football Association collective agreement, which is binding in French la, trainee players must accept offers from professional contracts with the club that trained them. if not, they must pay damages to that club equal to a minimum of one year's salary under the contract that was offered but declined. Advise Olivier as to whether he could rely upon EU law to resist an action for damages against him by his French club if he accepts the offer from the English club. (iii) Jean is a French national who has been living in Ireland for several years without ever having applied for a residence permit. He has no obvious financial means and appears to have lived from income made from occasional busking. He does not have health insurance. He is arrested by Gardaí on suspicion of terrorist offences, but released without charge. The security services in Northern Ireland had passed on information linking him to terrorist groups there. Advise the Irish Government on the compatibility with EU law of deporting Jean back to France.

Answer part (a) or (b). Do not attempt both parts. (a) By reference to the relevant case-law of the Court of Justice, analyse the scope of Treaty provisions on the free movement of capital and, in particular, the distinction between a restriction of that freedom and a restriction on the freedom to provide services and on the freedom of establishment. Which of the restrictions, if any, is the broader? (b) Under Article 67(4) TFEU "[t]he Union shall facilitate access to justice, in particular through the principle of mutual recognition of judicial and extrajudicial decisions in civil matters". Assess by reference particularly to Council Regulation (EC) No. 44/2001 on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgments in Civil and Commercial Matters ("the Brussels I Regulation") and Council Regulation (EC) 2201/2003 of 27 November 2003 on the recognition and enforcement of judgments in matrimonial matters and matters of parental responsibility ("the Brussels 2 bis Regulation"), as well as the case-law of the Court of Justice interpreting those Regulations, the extent to which the Union is achieving the objective of Article 67(4) TFEU.

questions.description: Ireland exempted from excise duty fossil fuels used for the production of… Advise the Irish government on whether there…
questions.text: Ireland exempted from excise duty fossil fuels used for the production of alumina in the Shannon region between 1983 and 2005. In 2005, the exemption was replaced with a reduced rate. The Commission, which had expressed concerns to the Government regarding the exemption, initiated in 2006 the procedure provided for in Article 108(2) TFEU (formerly Article 88(2) EC) with regard both to the exemption and reduced rate. In its resulting decision, the Commission has decided that both the exemption and reduced rate are new State aids that are incompatible with the common market. However, its decision only orders Ireland to recover the difference between the reduced rat and the normal rate of excise duty from the date of introduction of the reduced rate in 2005. Advise the Irish government on whether there are grounds to challenge the Commission decision before the Union judicature. Explain the procedure to be followed for such a challenge. Is there a risk that the Commission decision on recovery could be challenged as being too lenient before the Union judicature and, if so, by whom?

Answer part (A) [or]{.underline} part (B). Do not attempt both parts. (A) Analyse and explain the contribution made over the years by the Union judicature to the development of a doctrine of fundamental rights protection in the Union legal order. [Or]{.underline} (B) Critically assess the seminal judgment of the Court of Justice in Joined Cases C-402/06 P and C-415/05 P kadi and AI Barakaat International Foundation v Council {2008} ECR I-6351. Has the Court effectively disregarded the primacy of international law over domestic law in striving to assert the autonomy of Union law and to safeguard rights as protected under Union law?

Answer part (A) [or]{.underline} part (B). Do not attempt both parts. (A) Having particular regard to Articles 289 to 291 TFEU and relevant case-law, critically assess the role of the Council, the Commission and the European Parliament in the "ordinary legislative procedure", as well as the role of the Commission in the adoption of "delegated" and "implementing" acts. Based on your assessment, would you say that there is a discernible separation of legislative and executive powers in the EU system? [Or]{.underline} (B) The Commission adopted in April 2011 a (fictitious) "green paper" whereon it is now seeking observations on a proposed directive to harmonise certain aspects of the laws and regulations of the Member States relating to civil marriages. The objective of the draft directive would be: "to facilitate the functioning of the internal market by harmonising those aspects of diverging national marriage laws most likely to affect the unhindered free movement of persons within the internal market". The directive would concentrate on the formal requirements for contracting a valid civil marriage by proposing a minimum age of consent of 18 years for heterosexual couples and 21 for homosexual couples. However, it would also require the following substantive rights, which are currently only granted in some Member States, to be guaranteed by all Member States: (ii) community of property rights between spouses in respect of movable and immovable (real and personal) property acquired by either spouse after the marriage. The proposed legal basis for the draft is Article 114 TFEU. In the alternative, the commission proposes Article 352 TFEU as the legal basis. As an expert in EU law you have been asked by the Attorney General to consider whether the Union legislature would have competence to adopt such a directive on the bases proposed by the Commission or at all. By reference, in particular, to relevant Court of Justice case-law concerning the scope of articles 114 and 352 TFEU, prepare a legal opinion setting out your advice.

questions.description: Solidarity is a trade union representing mostly lower paid public sector… Directive.
questions.text: Solidarity is a trade union representing mostly lower paid public sector workers. It seeks advice on behalf of a number of its members who are non-established civil servants (hereinafter "the members") and who, between 30 September 2003 and 30 September 2011, were given a number of successive fixed term contracts of varying durations by several Government departments, under which their terms and conditions as regards pay and pensions in particular were less favourable than those of established civil servants doing comparable work. Under Article 4 of a 1999 EC Directive, discriminatory treatment regarding employment conditions of fixed-term contracts by comparison with permanent employees is prohibited "unless the difference in treatment can be justified on objective grounds". Article 5 of the same Directive, and with the aim of preventing abuse, required Member States to introduce "one or more of the following measures". (i) an obligation for employers to offer fixed-term employees a permanent position after three years unless there are "objective reasons justifying the renewal" of the relevant fixed-term employment contract; (ii) a maximum total duration of successive fixed-term employment contracts; (iii) a limit on the number of permissible renewals of such contracts. The Directive should have been implemented by 30 September 2001. Ireland, however, only implemented the Directive by a 2009 Act of the Oireachtas, the commencement of which was postponed until 30 September 2011. Advise Solidarity, by reference to relevant EU law principles and case-law, whether the members: (i) may rely directly on Article 4 of the 1999 Directive in challenging their less favourable employment conditions; (ii) may rely on Article 5 to challenge the successive renewal of their contracts between 30 September 2001 and 30 September 2011; and (iii) are obliged to introduce, so as to pursue the claims referred to in points (i) and (ii) above, individual legal proceedings before the ordinary Irish courts, in circumstances where Solidarity could bring a representative action on their behalf before the labour court pursuant to the 2009 Act (which permits such representative cases and which provides that, if unsuccessful in such representative actions, trade unions will not be exposed to an order for costs in favour of the defendant employer) if the claims being pursued were based on the rights conferred by the 2009 Act of Irish law more generally rather than on the Directive.

questions.description: Write a note on any three of the following seven cases, setting out their core facts, the key issues of EU law that…
questions.text: Write a note on any three of the following seven cases, setting out their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by EU courts: (i) Case 26/62 Van Gend en Loos (1963) ECR 1; (ii) Case 152/84 Marshall v. Southampton Area Health Authority (No.1)(1986) ECR 723; (iii) Case C-50/00 P Union de Pequenos Agricultores (UPA) V. Council (2002) ECR I-6677; (iv) Case C-200/02 Zhu & Chen (2004) ECR I-9925; (v) Case T-201/04 Microsoft v. Commission (2007) ECR II-3601; (vi) Case C-118/08 Transportes Urbanos y Servicios Generales v. Aministration del Estado (2010) ECR I-635; (vii) Case C-550/07 P Akzo Nobel Chemicals v. Commission (2010) ECR I-NYR (Judgment of 14 September 2010)

questions.description: Consider and advise in both of the following situations: (i) Mary is a… Consideration.
questions.text: Consider and advise in both of the following situations: (i) Mary is a waitress working for a restaurant on a 6-month temporary contract of employment. Just as her contract is due to expire; she discovers that she is pregnant. On informing her employer, it decides not to renew her contract on its expiry. As Mary would be taking maternity leave in the following 6 months, the employer considers that she be unable to fulfil the terms of a new contract. Advise Mary as to whether the refusal to renew her contract under these circumstances is contrary to EU sex discrimination law. [And]{.underline} (ii) Joan has worked as a manager for a health authority for the past five years. Previously, following her graduation from university, she worked in the private sector for several years before becoming a homemaker for a period of six years. She has discovered that a number of male colleagues, who hold similar managerial positions to hers, but for periods of between two and four years, are paid more than her. In response to Joan's complaint, the health authority argues that the differences in pay are justified by the male comparators' longer service records with the authority, notwithstanding that the service was mostly at lower non-managerial grades. Furthermore, Joan is informed that her previous private sector work experience cannot be taken into consideration because of her six-year career break. However, career breaks of up to three years do not preclude previous experience being taken into account. Two of her male colleagues, whose previous work experience has been taken into account, took three year career breaks to "travel around the world". Advise Joan as to the lawfulness of the authority's use of the criterion of length of service as a determinant of pay under EU sex discrimination law, in particular Article 157 TFEU, and its refusal, based on its three-year rule, to consider her previous work experience.

questions.description: Consider and advise in each of the following situations: (i) A Swedish… Advise him as to his chances of success under…
questions.text: Consider and advise in each of the following situations: (i) A Swedish trade union is boycotting the provision of building services by FE-1, an Estonian building contractor, because FE-1 is paying its workers less than the minimum wage agreed in the collective agreement that binds Swedish employers and trade unions in the building industry. Swedish labour law permits trade unions to engage in boycotting, which is seen as a legitimate form of collective action, in order to protect the collectively agreed Swedish minimum wage, whenever an employer, whether Swedish or foreign, has not accepted the relevant collective agreement. FE-1 is paying its workers posted to Sweden marginally over the minimum hourly rate for building workers in Sweden in just under half of the relevant collectively agreed Swedish rate. Advise FE-1 as to whether it could challenge the collective boycott in Sweden based on EU law. (ii) Helmut resides in Hamburg, Germany, but holidays regularly in Austria. Helmut is severely disabled and cannot travel on his own by public transport and so, as a single person, normally uses a specially adapted car for travelling. On being subject to a random control in his car on a tolled section of an Austrian motorway, Helmut is found not to be displaying a toll disc for the motorway, which, under Austrian law must be purchased by all motorway users prior to using such tolled sections. Toll discs are issued free of charge to disabled persons who are resident or ordinarily resident in Austria. Helmut has been fined for not displaying the disc and to appeal. Advise him as to his chances of success under EU law. (iii) Mary is a dual British and Irish national, who has lived all her life in the United Kingdom. She marries Bob, a Jamaican national, whilst on holiday in Jamaica. Bob is refused a right of residence in the UK under UK immigration law. Advise Mary and Bob on whether EU law affords them a right of residence in the UK.

Answer Part (A) [or]{.underline} Part (B). Do not attempt both parts. (A) Assess critically the interpretation, in the case-law of the Union judicature, of the following important concepts under Article 101 TFEU: <!-- --> (i) "agreements", (ii) "undertakings", (iii) "concerted practices". What in particular, are the critical distinguishing factors between anti-competitive "agreement(s) between undertakings" and "concerted practices" involving undertakings? How has the Union judicature interpreted the requirement that such agreements or practices must "affect trade between Member States" EU competition law will apply? [Or]{.underline} (B) By reference to relevant case-law of the Union judicature concerning Article 102 TFEU, analyse the extent to which a dominant undertaking may take steps to protect its own commercial position in the market on which it is dominant or on a related market if its position is threatened by a rival and/or new entrant. Could such steps or protective behaviour be acceptable if its purpose is specifically to strengthen or extend the dominant market position of the dominant undertaking in question? [And]{.underline} Indicate, giving reasons for your answer, whether your advices would be different if John's will was invalid and he therefore died intestate. Note that John's parents have predeceased him and his three brothers are his next of kin. 8 marks

questions.description: Mary died recently at the age of 92.
questions.text: Mary died recently at the age of 92. She lived the final years of her life in a state of some confusion and fear for her personal safety. Her house is located in a quiet rural spot and Mary was often petrified by news reports of attacks on elderly persons in circumstances similar to her own. Thus, she was very relieved when Betty, a young neighbour, began to visit her on a regular basis. Betty began to take charge of the day to day running of Mary's life and, having gained Mary's confidence, suggested to her that she should transfer the house to Betty. Betty argued that if she did this Mary would acquire peace of mind and freedom from constant worry about intruders. Before making any decision, Mary spoke to her solicitor who advised strongly against the transfer to Betty. Despite this advice, Mary, being timid in nature and not wishing to offend Betty or lose her companionship, transferred the property into Betty's name. Mary's nephew, Conor, to whom Mary left all of her property by will, now wishes to challenge the transfer to Betty and seeks your opinion on the matter.
